storage_extensions.usage: mmap both files; 64 threads each scan a contiguous row range of these sorted arrays; accumulate sum per orderkey using register variables (cur_key, cur_sum); disjoint key ranges per thread means no accumulator array and no merge phase; handle ~64 boundary keys in post-processing

join_order.notes: Direct O(1) index lookup for each of the ~2520 qualifying orderkeys. orders_by_orderkey[okey] = row index into orders column files. 2520 DRAM-latency accesses (~252 us) into page-cached index.
Dense array 5.7MB fully L3-resident; only ~2520 lookups total — negligible cost.

filters.predicate: SUM(l_quantity) GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM > 300
filters.notes: Applied during sorted scan: key transitions signal group end; emit qualifying keys in the same pass. ~2520 qualifying orderkeys from 15M distinct.

data_structures.pass1_accumulator: register_variables(cur_key: int32_t, cur_sum: int32_t) per thread — no array, no hash table; O(1) memory per thread; key transitions detected by sorted order
data_structures.boundary_keys: std::pair<int32_t,int32_t>[64] per thread for first and last key partial sums; post-merged in O(64) after all threads finish
data_structures.qualifying_keys_with_sums: std::vector<std::pair<int32_t,int32_t>>(~2520 entries) of (orderkey, sum_qty) pairs where sum > 300; built by each thread for its interior keys plus boundary merge

parallelism.pass1_sorted_scan: 64 threads, each processes a contiguous row range [lo, hi) of the sorted arrays; within each chunk all rows for the same orderkey are contiguous (since sorted); thread emits interior qualifying (key, sum) pairs immediately; stores partial sums for first_key and last_key of its chunk for boundary reconciliation after thread join
parallelism.boundary_merge: sequential post-processing of 64 boundary pairs after all threads join; for each adjacent pair (thread t last_key == thread t+1 first_key): combine sums, check > 300; trivial cost
parallelism.pass2_direct_lookup: sequential over ~2520 qualifying keys; no parallelism needed

indexes.notes: File: orders/orders_by_orderkey.bin. Layout: int32_t[60000001], array[okey] = row index into orders column files, sentinel -1. 240MB file; MADV_RANDOM; 2520 cache-miss accesses into page cache.
File: customer/customer_by_custkey.bin. Layout: int32_t[1500001], array[ckey] = row index into customer column files, sentinel -1. 5.7MB, fully L3-resident.

fields.name: o_orderdate
fields.source: orders/o_orderdate.bin[orders_by_orderkey[okey]] (days_since_epoch; decode to YYYY-MM-DD for output only)

optimization_notes: Root cause is a storage-level constraint (Category E): lineitem is physically sorted by l_shipdate, so l_orderkey values appear in random order during any sequential scan. Every approach that uses an accumulator array indexed by l_orderkey (iter_0's 64 × 229MB thread-local arrays, iter_1's shared atomic array, iter_2's int16_t arrays) suffers random-write cache misses into a structure much larger than the 44MB LLC, and must either pay the merge cost (73ms at 200 GB/s) or atomic contention. The fix is a sorted copy of (l_orderkey, l_quantity) ordered by l_orderkey, built once and stored persistently. With sorted input, Pass 1 becomes a streaming group-by: each thread scans its contiguous row range sequentially with only two register variables (cur_key, cur_sum) — no accumulator array, no merge phase. Sequential access at 286MB / 200 GB/s ≈ 1.4ms. The ~64 boundary keys (one per thread boundary, since max 7 lineitems per order) are reconciled in O(1) post-processing. Pass 2 uses the pre-built orders_by_orderkey index for 2520 direct O(1) lookups instead of a 15M-row orders scan, matching the approach attempted in iter_2 but now viable since the dominant merge cost is eliminated. Total expected hot time: ~2-4ms versus 66ms baseline.
